item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What an awsome idea! My +1 for sure!
Very well written too

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this has probably already been said, and I don't know if was your intention, but:

The 'Europe' lines. When he asks if we have a Europe in this universe? If I'm reading this SCP correctly, the use of the languages from that universe causes the other universe to change to suit its version of reality, right? So we only call it Europe because he called it that… so who knows what it used to be?

May not be right, but in my head it works and thus is deserving of my +1.

item.title: Pretty much the same as everyone else
item.content:encoded: Hooked me with the concept, doesn't seem to go anywhere though. The scp itself seems kind of childish in nature. It could work if it was worked into the fact that since only one person speaks it, it doesn't develop quickly or something along those lines but as is I have to go neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept and the way the interview is executed. But I’m afraid I have to echo what everyone else said: it doesn’t feel complete. Maybe because there’s really no reason to doubt the Foundation’s assessment that it’s pretty much harmless in our reality and thus the whole thing seems like it would be just a bit of trivia in-universe. Because of that there seems to be no conclusion.

item.title: Are you stupid? Can you give me some statements?
item.content:encoded: This made me lol hard. Solid +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, another potential idea here is to riff off of the theory that we can only think about that which we have words for. If that were the case, and this is a sentient language that can change word meanings, it could therefore change people's thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fresh.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got the weirdest upvote right now.

I really don't understand why I like this so much, and yet I do. I want a lot more of this.

I also am considering whether a cross test with 1139 would be fun. Probably not, but I'll consider it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had your job
Well it’s what you get for unauthorized use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This… Im not sure what to think of it. It certainly has my interest, but why is this realities Dr. Green not allowed to speak with the SCP Dr. Green? She seems reasonable enough to be trusted around another version of her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I was reading this I was wondering if the language was Etruscan and the "siblings" were the other Tyrrhenian languages. I like the ambiguity though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Language Wars: Revenge of the Etruscan. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very good idea and well-written, though I agree with others it seems it doesn't quite go all the way with the concept, and is a slightly flat ending. Nevertheless, well worth an up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also felt like the fact that this is a self-aware, self-modifying, intentionally manipulative language wasn't exploited enough.

Let's say that it doesn't just modify itself, but that it also can modify other languages co-located in the same brain. Now we've got to question every piece of information provided by a speaker, including all of its effects and history.

So now you're set up for a 3rd act twist where this is discovered, and that we don't know how much of what we (the readers) know is false. This could be played with quite a bit to great effect, and it could even be that the whole language thing is a bluff, and that it's actually a verbally transmitted memetic effect that has already been transmitted to the other researchers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had the same thought!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very clever concept that doesn't go quite as far as it could. The interview log about the rousing speech is hugely promising but also the best part of the piece; nonetheless, I enjoyed this enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Easiest +1 I've given in a while. Deeply clever and with strong writing to boot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely astounding. It's a deeply clever concept, executed authentically and bringing an interesting character along with it, to boot.

If you have any tales you'd like to tell with SCP-3027-1, I'd love to read them.

An enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, that wasn't my intention, but since it's not Indo-European that is possible…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this, but was I the only one who saw the reference to northern Italy and immediately thought 'Etruscan!'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had the researcher bring it to our world to study the language in a relatively controlled, low-risk environment. This thing is really powerless if only one person speaks it, and studying anomalies is a big part of the Foundation's MO.

As for the 'do something horrible,' I think this language accomplishing its goal would be horrible enough; for one thing, it would basically control all human thought, and also it would erase entire cultures. (Worth noting that SCP-2112 is a Keter for approximately that reason.)

And yeah, I'm starting to regret not having the grammar book breach containment— I described it in detail and everything, it really would have made a good Chekhov's gun! I might revise this at some point to have more action in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I missed it but at least to me its unclear why the researcher brought it to our reality. Were they trying to escape it or was it going to do something horrible once it had control of the world?

Like the others I really like the concept but feel like it needs a little more of an ending or content to really shine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 from me, with notes :)

As I began reading the collapsibles, I had a sinking feeling in my chest that said "Oh, this isn't going to go well." Our Foundationeers were way too cocky and with something as…intangible and adaptive and um, cognitively fluid as 3027 is, they were asking for A Bad Time. Buuuut, nothing happens and the interviews just kind of come to an end, with no real narrative conclusion or hook or "moment". I know not every skip HAS to end on one, but this one seemed to be headed towards a more, I guess, satisfying ending.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I created both of the images myself.

Edit: I realize now that saying that is incorrect. I created the first image (consonant inventory) entirely by myself, and I created the second (vowel inventory) by editing an image of a blank vowel trapezoid on Wikimedia Commons.

Original source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Blank_vowel_trapezoid.png
Creator: Denelson83

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately, I didn't enjoy this. I find the base concept quite interesting, but the execution is lacking, especially in the interview logs. The language used by the Foundation researchers during the interviews feels a little unprofessional, such as the final line of the last interview. It doesn't mesh well with how I'd imagine they act.

The narrative route of the anomaly seeking to destroy all other languages feels a little pedestrian, a pretty standard, straightforward development, and the reveal of its desire for vengeance against the loss of its 'brethren' fell flat for me. It feels like an afterthought of a conclusion - it's playing the narrative too safely, in my eyes.

The second interview seems fairly pointless to me. The danger the anomaly poses is already made apparent in both the description and the first interview, so the Euclid-Keter discussion just seems like entirely extraneous detail, especially considering that it isn't brought up again. It can be summed up like this: "The anomaly is really dangerous!" "Eh, kind of."

I honestly thought this was building up to a containment breach, or some kind of scenario where the Foundation pays for its negligence. Instead, it just fizzles out.

On the other hand, like I said, I like the base idea, as well as the tie-in to SCP-3022 and alternate realities. The containment procedures, especially the first paragraph, serve as a well-crafted hook, so kudos for that.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

EDIT: all good

item.title: Re: review
item.content:encoded: Oh, I know. LilyFlower just beat me to it. Edit: oh that wasn't addressed to me lol

item.title: Re: review
item.content:encoded: You can just add the rating module yourself. It is an acceptable edit by a non-author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Total honesty: I upvoted immediately for the consonant and vowel inventories. The rest of the article could have just been "herpa derp derp" over and over and I would have had no regrets. But this is damned interesting, if maybe hanging a little too much on another skip, and definitely a solid hit as far as linguistic skips go. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, is anyone else unable to see the images, or is that just me? I should note that I can see them on mobile, just not on my computer, for some reason.

Edit: I cleared my browser cache and now it's working fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really on the fence about this one. As an overall concept, it's interesting, but the concept that it changes the meaning of the language to meet its own ends seems to suggest that most people would have abandoned the language the moment it became an unreliable form of communication; the moment it was clear you could speak a language that gave you no agency, it seems likely you'd give it up unless the changes were extraordinarily subtle. The interview log does not give the indication that it's particularly subtle, so it's a hard sell.

This also seems like an anomaly that could be easily contained by amnesticizing or isolating 3027-1 and securing the dictionary. Interview log 3027-b sort of lampshades this by having the doctor and 3027-1 discuss how easy it would be to eliminate any risk of the language spreading. Likewise, there's no reason to have images of 3027's linguistic maps be unclassified images in the file, so the included images seem to represent the kind of cognitohazard that would be redacted in most containment procedures.

It's not quite a downvote because the idea is fully formed and well written, but it does stretch plausibility pretty far to believe that this language managed to dominate an entire reality and the Foundation's containment procedures seem suspect as described here. No vote for me.

item.title: Re: review
item.content:encoded: Thanks for fixing it, that was my bad (Edit: actually thanks to LilyFlower)

item.title: review
item.content:encoded: No rating module :P
